text: preparation, like you can navigate it and get the information that you wanted. So it's sort of been the way that we now use to communicate internally using these different applications. So the, I think it was almost the expectation is that you always use some kind of AI tool to help your job, you know, be easier. And our CTO has this like famous saying, which he sent on stage for a few different presentations, is like ask your employees what you hate most about your job. And like the answer to that is what you should build. and yes, you said at our level, maybe slightly less manual work. So, you know, we might not be the best people to come up with ideas. But if you ask the people that actually day in and day out, perhaps do, you know, sort of very repetitive, mundane work, you can have a pretty good list for you of some stuff that can probably automate it using an agent or, you know, at least improved by building some kind of special interface for you to interact with. Yes, definitely hearing a lot of efficiency gains, automation, you know, make things operationally easier, but what kind of gets you excited about what could be coming up and be enabled over the next two or three years where we had it? Yes, I think for me, we have this framework internally, which is like deterministic and non-deterministic workflows and simple and complex. And we've basically done a pretty good job in three out of the four quadrants. The one we haven't quite, and this is not for sale, I think this is like the world, haven't quite captured yet is non-deterministic and complex. So like, why did churn increase last month? Or why did signups improve? Well, you're not asking a specific answer to like, was this above or below targets? Or is this in policy? Or can you draft me like a standard template NDA, right? You have to dig into different systems. You don't even know where to even look for the answer. And it's not even a specific answer. You have to look for multiple things. and how do you even know, how does even the AI know when to stop searching that they've sort of reached the end of potential solutions? So as I think about what could be, you know, improvements coming, and, you know, ChatGPT has really only been around like two and a half-ish years since 3.5 came out, and we've seen tremendous improvements in those two and a half years. Imagine what would happen in another two and a half years. I think we're just going to see exponential improvements in these models and capabilities. So I think really good, non-deterministic, complex workflows I think would be a sort of a game changer for finance teams to provide insights to the rest of the organization Yeah I really like that kind of saying of like the human brain literally cannot comprehend an exponential function. And I can just see that from kind of two, three years ago, even kind of six, seven months ago from within SeatGeek having kind of no internally developed AI for the office of finance. Now we have kind of nine, 10 agents kind of building a new one every week or so. And then I think our first experience, I think, with AI was actually with Brex and with the AI assistant and kind of seeing kind of that operational leverage that you can get when the agent can go out and kind of look at that anomaly and kind of reach out to the person. So we've been able to, I think, at least keep headcount closer to constant the last few years from a G&A perspective as kind of the business keeps getting bigger. I think to echo your point, Martin, I think one of the things that I'm really interested to see is how can AI get to a place with the right structured data sets and such that it can answer those more amorphous prompt questions. Because I think what hopefully finance and people can still do better than AI in the fullness of time is knowing what questions to ask. What is the thing that you need to know about your business to make decisions? And having that kind of innate sense. So you may know, hey, why is Churn doing this? And what should we do to drive this in a better direction? When I ask that of my team today, it's like, we'll have to go circle back. We're going to have to go look at the data. We're going to have to go spend time in cycles to understand the full root cause of this issue to then come back with a proposal. And if AI is able to speed up those cycle times, I think that would be a huge unlock for sure. I was asking the questions to ask, but also to quality check the answer. Yeah. Right. Like my doctor told me that she's allowed to use chat GPT to ask for medical advice, but I'm not because she can look at the answer and say, like, that is just dead wrong. Yeah, it's hallucinating. You're not going to die in the next 45 minutes. Whereas if someone that does not sort of understand the business might ask why it's churn up and it would just say, oh, there's more days in October than September. And you'd be like, well, that's not definitely not the answer. Right. But that could be something that the LLM would say. So to be able to sort of know what questions to ask, and also sort of sense check, does this answer actually make sense? Yeah. So Ben, back to you. So thinking about your, zooming out to your finance stacks you got kind of a best of breed and kind of a lot of apps You also developing a lot internally When you out there looking to solve a certain problem and you looking at vendors you thinking about, you know, building it yourself internally. What's that? What are kind of that pros and cons list? Like, what are you out and looking? How do you find out what's going to be that killer app that's going to help you? Yeah. So I think for us, we have very much a build and buy mindset when it comes to all these tools and applications because I think the way we think about it is like where do we believe we can drive the greatest amount of ROI as it relates to like what we should be building internally. So when we think about that, for example, we decided to onboard, we decided to automate our onboarding flow for customers. And that was something that we were like, okay, if we sort of combine our know your customer workflow and model with our underwriting credit model, we can have a better customer experience because we can more seamlessly ingest both the structured data of the application flow, but also behavioral and risk signals to be able to automate a lot of the onboarding that our customers go through. So that was like something we decided to build internally because it's so unique to us and it leverages so much of our own data to be able to sort of create a better customer experience that we're like, we have to build this ourselves. It would be very hard for us to outsource that or buy a product that otherwise has that capability. On the flip side, when we think about, say, our customer service agents, we use Sierra AI. We've done a really deep integration with them, and they are now sort of through CRI's product and the integration with Brex, they now handle most of our inbound sort of customer service cases. And so from that perspective, like that was something that we're like, look, we can obviously surface up to them the detail and the context that they need to have the agent respond on our behalf, but that isn't sort of the actual customer service agent in and of itself. It doesn't necessarily have to be something that like Brex uniquely builds because we actually think that there are other people who are, That's their entire business model, and they're probably better set up to do so based on not just Brexit's instance, but they'll obviously have customers in other industries and other customers in general that they will be able to feed into a better product experience for that agent. And so I think on the finance side specifically, there is some platforms that we use, for example, these numeric that helps us with our BVA and cash flux analysis that we do every month. We feel like that's a pretty good tool for us to have bought by virtue of its integration with NetSuite. And just like, it's something that we didn't feel we had a unique ability to sort of build it in a better way. So we like let use that other tool because whilst the tools and to your point about like small software while a lot of these tools and agents are very easy to build you also have to to a certain extent maintain them and you have to make sure that they kept up to date and that they continue to advance And so I think when you think about which sort of systems to build yourself there is also an inherent tax to that because you're supporting it, which is, obviously, if you buy a tool, there's an inherent cost of paying a vendor on an ongoing basis. But one of the things that I would say we've made historically, Brex kind of, we grew up in the sort of zero income, sorry, zero interest rate period. and we also were zero income as well like you know we just decided to build everything we're like oh we can build everything cash is free like let's just go out and go crazy and then what we sort of have learned over the years is like we actually need to be much more intentional about where do we believe we're uniquely positioned to do a better job building this versus going to an outside party because of all those other costs that are incurred yeah as I said we like to build stuff we also recognize when there are pre-existing tools that are really good so like for legal views like GCAI we didn't feel the need to reinvent the wheel for something that is specifically trained on legal stuff. We do also really good integrations with existing system of records. So for Ironclad, for example, we use Ironclad AI. And I've done two fundraisers at Vercel. And the first time around, we got some questions from the investors of how many of your contracts are in the event of an M&A, they don't roll over. And we had to literally hire a law firm to go through every single customer contract and give us that number. Probably cost us $50,000 or something like that. And with Ironclad AI, it was just a prompt. And it created a list of the customers where they have the right to end the contract in the event of M&A. So that went from a huge pain. It was over a weekend, poor associates at this law firm, to a quick prompt of all the data. Now there's some work put into that. We've got to make sure all the contracts are in Ironclad and stuff. But if the AI works really well with the underlying data, then that's also worth it. That just reminded me, just flashbacks of the same exercise that I've done and other various financings and that tagging and the hundreds and hundreds of... I asked you a follow-up question after you had done it, which was like, and how many of them have non-standard payment terms? And we just said, we're not going to answer that. Which would have been another easy prompt on Ironclad. I will say, investor due diligence is a huge, it's so much easier now with AI. Because as soon as, especially if you've gone deep, really deep with one investor, and they've asked you,
